class_schedule.topic: What Is Security? Traditional and Non-Traditional Approaches
learning_objectives: Define security and distinguish between traditional and human security approaches
Identify the levels and referent objects of security analysis
Survey the evolution of security studies from Cold War to contemporary frameworks
readings: Buzan, Waever & de Wilde, Security: A New Framework for Analysis Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Security is the most contested concept in international studies. Who is to be secured, against what threats, and by what means are all deeply political questions.
key_concepts: Traditional security: military threats to state sovereignty
Human security: freedom from fear and freedom from want
Securitization theory: security as a speech act (Copenhagen School)
Levels of analysis: individual, national, regional, international, global
Sectors: military, political, economic, societal, environmental
lecture_notes.demonstration: Securitization exercise: analyzing how climate change has been framed as a security threat

review.value: The Copenhagen School concept that an issue becomes a 'security' issue when a securitizing actor successfully frames it as an existential threat requiring extraordinary measures
The UNDP framework shifting the referent object of security from the state to the individual, encompassing economic, food, health, environmental, personal, community, and political security

learning_objectives: Explain the logic of nuclear deterrence and mutually assured destruction
Evaluate contemporary nuclear threats and arms control
readings: Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT) Articles I-VI

lecture_notes.introduction: Nuclear weapons transformed international politics by making great power war potentially suicidal. Deterrence theory and arms control remain central to global security.
key_concepts: Deterrence theory: capability, credibility, communication
Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD) and strategic stability
The NPT regime: three pillars of non-proliferation, disarmament, peaceful use
Proliferation challenges: North Korea, Iran, nuclear terrorism
Arms control: New START, INF Treaty collapse, future prospects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deterrence scenario analysis: evaluating credibility of extended deterrence in East Asia

review.value: The condition where both sides in a nuclear dyad can absorb a first strike and retaliate with devastating force, theoretically making nuclear war irrational for both
Non-proliferation (non-nuclear states forswear weapons), disarmament (nuclear states commit to reduce arsenals), peaceful use (all states can access civilian nuclear technology)

learning_objectives: Define terrorism and analyze the challenges of defining it
Explain the causes of terrorism through multiple theoretical lenses
Evaluate counterterrorism strategies and their trade-offs

lecture_notes.introduction: Terrorism is a tactic, not an ideology. Understanding why groups choose it and how states respond is essential for security analysis.
key_concepts: Definitional challenges: one person's terrorist is another's freedom fighter
Types: religious, ethno-nationalist, left-wing, right-wing, lone wolf
Root causes debate: grievance vs. strategic choice vs. radicalization pathways
Counterterrorism approaches: military, intelligence, law enforcement, CVE
Civil liberties trade-offs in counterterrorism
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative case analysis: IRA, al-Qaeda, and ISIS as different models of terrorist organization

review.value: Terrorism as a rational strategy by weak actors to coerce stronger opponents through spectacular violence, provoke overreaction, and attract attention to their cause
Programs aimed at preventing radicalization through community engagement, deradicalization, and addressing underlying grievances, complementing military and intelligence approaches

class_schedule.topic: Civil War, Peacekeeping, and Post-Conflict Reconstruction
learning_objectives: Analyze the causes of civil wars: greed, grievance, and opportunity
Evaluate the evolution and effectiveness of UN peacekeeping

lecture_notes.introduction: Since the Cold War, civil wars have been the dominant form of armed conflict. Understanding them and how the international community responds is a central security challenge.
key_concepts: Causes of civil war: greed (resources), grievance (inequality), opportunity (weak states)
Evolution of peacekeeping: traditional, multidimensional, robust
Peacekeeping effectiveness: evidence from quantitative and qualitative studies
Peacebuilding: DDR, SSR, transitional justice, institution-building
The responsibility to protect (R2P) and its limits
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study: evaluating the UN peacekeeping mission in Sierra Leone (UNAMSIL)

review.value: Collier argues economic factors (resource predation, diaspora financing) better explain civil wars than political grievances, though critics contend grievances remain fundamental

The 2005 principle that sovereignty entails a responsibility to protect populations from genocide, war crimes, ethnic cleansing, and crimes against humanity, with international intervention as a last resort

class_schedule.topic: Cybersecurity and Space as New Domains of Conflict
learning_objectives: Analyze cyberspace as a domain of interstate competition and conflict
Evaluate the challenges of applying existing legal frameworks to cyber operations
Assess the militarization of outer space and arms control prospects
readings: Rid, Cyber War Will Not Take Place Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: New technological domains are transforming the security landscape. Cyber operations and space warfare present challenges that existing security frameworks struggle to address.
key_concepts: Cyber operations: espionage, sabotage, subversion, and their thresholds
Attribution problem: difficulty of identifying attackers in cyberspace
Deterrence in cyberspace: why traditional deterrence logic is difficult to apply
Tallinn Manual: applying international law to cyber operations
Space security: satellites, anti-satellite weapons, space debris
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tabletop exercise: responding to a cyberattack on critical infrastructure
setup_requirements: Response options matrix (technical, diplomatic, military, legal)

review.value: The difficulty of definitively identifying the perpetrator of a cyberattack, which undermines deterrence because states cannot credibly threaten retaliation against unknown attackers
A non-binding academic study applying existing international law to cyber operations, concluding that the law of armed conflict applies but identifying significant ambiguities

learning_objectives: Analyze the security implications of climate change
Evaluate pandemic preparedness as a global security issue
Assess how non-traditional threats challenge existing security institutions
readings: CNA Military Advisory Board, National Security and the Accelerating Risks of Climate Change (excerpt)
WHO, A World at Risk: Global Preparedness Monitoring Board Report (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Climate change and pandemics represent existential threats that traditional military security frameworks are poorly equipped to address. Adapting security thinking is urgent.
key_concepts: Climate-security nexus: resource scarcity, migration, state fragility, conflict
Climate adaptation as security investment
Pandemic preparedness: the global health security architecture
COVID-19 lessons: failure of early warning, supply chain vulnerability, vaccine equity
Biosecurity: natural, accidental, and deliberate biological threats
lecture_notes.demonstration: Scenario planning: security implications of a 3-degree warming scenario in a selected region

review.value: The pathways through which climate change exacerbates security risks: resource scarcity, food insecurity, displacement, competition over water and land, and state fragility
The framework for preventing, detecting, and responding to infectious disease threats that cross borders, requiring international cooperation and domestic capacity

questions.question: What is securitization theory and how does it differ from traditional approaches to defining security threats? Provide an example of a successful securitization of a non-military issue.
questions.answer: Securitization theory (Copenhagen School) argues that security is not an objective condition but a social construction: an issue becomes a security threat when a 'securitizing actor' (usually a government or leader) successfully frames it as an existential threat to a referent object, thereby justifying extraordinary measures beyond normal politics. This differs from traditional approaches that define threats objectively (military capabilities, intentions). An example: after 9/11, terrorism was securitized in ways that justified extraordinary measures including mass surveillance, indefinite detention, and preemptive war that would not have been accepted under normal politics.

questions.question: Explain the logic of Mutually Assured Destruction (MAD). Why might rational states still choose to build up nuclear arsenals beyond what is needed for assured destruction?
questions.answer: MAD is the condition where both sides in a nuclear dyad possess enough nuclear weapons to survive a first strike and retaliate with devastating force, making nuclear war suicidal for both and therefore deterred. States might build beyond MAD levels because: (1) counterforce targeting -- seeking ability to destroy the enemy's weapons before they launch, potentially gaining a first-strike advantage; (2) uncertainty about how many weapons would survive a first strike; (3) bureaucratic politics -- military services and weapons labs have institutional incentives to advocate for more weapons; (4) bargaining chips for future arms control negotiations; (5) technological competition and prestige. These drivers can produce arms races even when both sides recognize that MAD already exists.

rubric.key: Provides multiple valid reasons for over-building
Analysis demonstrates understanding of deterrence dynamics

quizzes.topic: Terrorism, Civil War, and Emerging Threats

questions.question: Explain the 'attribution problem' in cybersecurity. Why does it undermine traditional deterrence theory, and what strategies might states use to address it?
questions.answer: The attribution problem is the difficulty of definitively identifying who is responsible for a cyberattack. Attackers can route operations through multiple countries, use false flags, and exploit the inherent anonymity of the internet. This undermines traditional deterrence because deterrence requires the adversary to know that retaliation will follow an attack -- if you cannot identify the attacker, you cannot retaliate credibly. Strategies to address it include: investing in forensic attribution capabilities, building international norms requiring states to not allow attacks from their territory, developing collective attribution mechanisms (like NATO's collective judgment), and shifting to deterrence by denial (making systems resilient) rather than deterrence by punishment (threatening retaliation).

questions.question: What is the Responsibility to Protect (R2P) and what are its three pillars? Why has its implementation been controversial?
questions.answer: R2P is the principle adopted at the 2005 UN World Summit stating that sovereignty carries responsibility. Its three pillars: (1) each state has the responsibility to protect its populations from genocide, war crimes, ethnic cleansing, and crimes against humanity; (2) the international community has the responsibility to assist states in meeting this obligation; (3) if a state manifestly fails to protect its people, the international community should take collective action, including military intervention as a last resort. Implementation has been controversial because: the 2011 Libya intervention exceeded its civilian protection mandate and resulted in regime change, making Russia and China reluctant to authorize future interventions; selective application (intervention in Libya but not Syria) suggests political rather than humanitarian motivations; and sovereignty-conscious states fear R2P provides cover for great power interference.

rubric.key: Correctly defines R2P and its three pillars

major_assignments.title: Security Threat Assessment and Policy Brief
major_assignments.description: Select a contemporary security threat (traditional or non-traditional) and produce a professional threat assessment followed by a policy brief with recommendations for a specific government or international organization.
objectives: Conduct a rigorous threat assessment using security studies frameworks
Apply multiple analytical perspectives to a security challenge
Communicate complex security analysis to a professional audience
deliverables: Two-page policy brief for a specific decision-maker
Threat assessment matrix with probability and impact ratings

criteria: Clear identification and scoping of the threat
Rigorous assessment of capability, intent, and vulnerability
Diverse sources: academic, government, think tank, primary
Rigorous and evidence-based security threat assessment

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on security concepts, theories, and current threats

simulation_participation.description: Participation in tabletop exercises and security simulations

major_assignments.description: Security threat assessment and policy brief

midterm_examination.description: Exam on traditional and non-traditional security concepts

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam with scenario-based security analysis

course_policies.attendance: Attendance mandatory for all simulation and tabletop exercises. Absences result in zero for missed exercises.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers penalized 10% per day. Policy briefs cannot be submitted late.
course_policies.collaboration: Tabletop exercises are collaborative. All written analysis must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Proper citation of security analysis sources required. Classified or restricted material must not be used.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to security studies databases, think tank publications, and government reports through university library.
